userMessage:
Write a comprehensive deep reflection document about the Skills Routing Architecture implementation journey we just completed.

## Context
We implemented a 5-phase skills routing architecture that transforms StringRay from a simple plugin injection system into a full-blown orchestration framework with:
- Phase 1: Skill Registry Foundation (discovery, caching, persistence)
- Phase 2: Routing Enhancement (SkillMatcher, capability-based matching, keyword boosting)
- Phase 3: Agent Config Integration (SkillResolver, agent-skill bindings, YAML parsing)
- Phase 4: Processor Pipeline (SkillPipeline, stage execution, timeout handling)
- Phase 5: Hot Reload (SkillWatcher, fs.watch, debouncing)

## What Was Built
### New Modules Created
- src/skills/types.ts - TypeScript interfaces (SkillManifest, MCPServerConfig, etc.)
- src/skills/parser.ts - YAML frontmatter parser with custom indentation handling
- src/skills/discovery.ts - SkillDiscoveryService for filesystem scanning
- src/skills/registry.ts - SkillRegistry with cache persistence
- src/skills/matcher.ts - SkillMatcher with confidence-based routing
- src/skills/resolver.ts - SkillResolver for agent bindings
- src/skills/pipeline.ts - SkillPipeline and SkillPipelineStage
- src/skills/watcher.ts - SkillWatcher for hot reload
- src/skills/index.ts - Module exports

### CLI Commands Added
- src/cli/commands/skill-list.ts - Lists all 30 discovered skills
- src/cli/commands/agent-skills.ts - Shows agent-skill bindings
- Both commands working and tested

### Core Integration
- Boot orchestrator updated with initializeSkillDiscovery()
- Plugin integration with skill matching logs
- Context preservation bug fixed (file-based storage)

### Test Results
- All 185 tests passing
- File existence verified
- CLI integration verified
- Dist files compiled correctly
- Skills auto-discovered (30 skills, 29 with MCP)

## Technical Challenges Overcome

### 1. YAML Parser Evolution
The YAML parser went through multiple iterations to handle nested objects:
- First attempt: Simple line-by-line parsing failed on indentation
- Second attempt: Indentation-based stack parser created "_items" arrays
- Final solution: Stack-based indentation tracking with proper nested object handling

### 2. TypeScript Strictness
The tsconfig had exactOptionalPropertyTypes: true which required explicit undefined types:
- Had to update ALL interfaces in types.ts
- Example: category?: string | undefined instead of category?: string

### 3. OpenCode Sandboxing
Files written to .opencode/logs/ not visible to shell ls but ARE written:
- Investigation showed fs.existsSync() returns true and content is verifiable
- Root cause: Virtual filesystem or sandboxing between Node.js and shell
- Solution: Use project root for context files where both can access

### 4. Context Preservation Bug
Original user message was LOST between chat.message and tool.execute.before hooks:
- chat.message captures original user message
- We synthesize it into simpler prompts
- tool.execute.before receives OUR prompt, not original user intent
- Solution: File-based context storage via context-{sessionId}.json in project root

## Architectural Decisions

### Why Project Root for Context Files?
- Both Node.js AND shell can access project root
- .opencode/logs/ is sandboxed (not visible to ls)
- Workaround: Write context files to project root instead

### Why Custom YAML Parser?
- js-yaml libraries couldn't track indentation levels properly for nested objects
- Stack-based approach gives us full control

### Why getSkillRegistry() Singleton?
- Registry and matcher were importing from each other
- Created circular dependency risk
- Solution: Singleton pattern to ensure single initialization

## What StringRay Is Becoming

StringRay is evolving from a simple plugin injection system into a complete orchestration framework:

### What It Was
- Plugin injection that added Codex terms to system prompts
- Simple agent routing based on config
- Static skill definitions with no runtime discovery

### What It Is Now
- Full orchestration framework with 30+ auto-discovered skills
- Dynamic skill discovery and hot reload capability
- Agent-skill binding resolution and validation
- Capability-based routing with confidence scoring
- Processor pipeline integration for pre/post execution
- Context preservation across hook boundaries
- Deep reflection and documentation
- CLI commands for skills and agents
- Multi-agent coordination and delegation
- Knowledge graphs built on preserved context

### Why This Matters
Skills routing is now the foundation for:
- Intelligent task routing based on actual capabilities, not just static config
- Learning systems that can improve over time
- Dynamic adaptation to new capabilities
- Context awareness that spans entire sessions

## Document Details

File: docs/reflections/deep/stringray-evolution-saga-2026-03-25.md
Length: Target 8000+ words
Format: Use SAGA_TEMPLATE.md format for extended narrative journeys

Sections to include:
1. Executive Summary (500 words)
2. The Saga Begins (narrative opening)
3. Discovery Phase - The Realization (500 words)
4. Architectural Tensions (500 words)
5. Implementation Hell (1000 words)
6. The Parser Wars (800 words)
7. Routing Enlightenment (600 words)
8. Resolution and Integration (700 words)
9. The Discovery (500 words)
10. Context Crisis (800 words)
11. The Breakthrough (600 words)
12. Integration Victory (700 words)
13. Testing and Validation (500 words)
14. Push to Production (400 words)
15. Epilogue: What StringRay Is Becoming (600 words)

Tone: Narrative, reflective, emotional journey
Include: Code snippets showing evolution, specific error messages, emotional states

End with: Victory and philosophical reflection on what StringRay is becoming.
